10.1016/j.jmp.2021.102523
The concept of a  knowledge structure  was first defined in  Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  to be a set  
                         Q 
                       of “problems” together with a collection  
                         K 
                       of subsets of  
                         Q 
                       such that if  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                       then  
                         K 
                       is realizable as the precise set of problems in  
                         Q 
                       that a student could be capable of solving.  Knowledge spaces  are then defined as knowledge structures such that  
                         K 
                       is closed under union. Since the seminal work in  Doignon and Falmagne (1985) , knowledge space theory has received a substantial amount of interest from many researchers, and is the focus of attention across a significant body of literature (e.g.,  Cosyn & Uzun, 2009; Dowling, 1993; Düntsch & Gediga, 1995; Schrepp, 1997; Stefanutti & Koppen, 2003; Suck, 2003, 2004, 2011 , and references to many other related papers can be found in the extensive bibliography provided in  Falmagne, Albert, Doble, Eppstein, & Hu, 2013 ).

The present study shares a common feature with knowledge space theory in that it is concerned with students’ preparation to learn. The inception of knowledge space theory in  Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  was motivated by the desire to provide a more comprehensive description of a learner’s “knowledge state” than what is uncovered by assessment-metrics. In short, assessment-metrics are often used to categorize individuals according to “how much” they know, whereas knowledge space theory aims to evaluate individuals according to “what” they know.

While the origins of knowledge space theory can be traced to student assessment, the concepts introduced in the current investigation are based on curriculum assessment. Instead of gauging the effectiveness of a curriculum according to “how much” success is achieved, curricula are evaluated based on their structural properties that affect “what” success may be achieved. Sets of tasks serve as “courses” of a curriculum, 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         Thus, regarding  
                               Q 
                             as a set of tasks, a direct connection with knowledge space theory is given in that it is impossible to complete every task in a course contained in  
                               Q 
                             unless the course is a subset of  
                               K 
                             for some  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                            . 
                       learners are identified with sets of “cognitive experience”, and mild assumptions are imposed that lead to the existence of a structure that encodes the interactions between learners and curricula. The theory accepts cognitive experience as an attribute of learning-preparedness (e.g., it admits the possibility that two learners who are able to complete the same tasks in a course need not be equivalently prepared to complete subsequent tasks), and it acknowledges the existence of “non-prerequisite” tasks that may facilitate preparedness. Furthermore, arguments are given to suggest that the purely mathematical theory inspired by the applications is interesting in its own right.

During the last century, academia has experienced an increase in demands from accrediting bodies to implement designs of curriculum assessment that address student-achievement (a brief history on accreditation in the United States is given in  Ewell, 2015 ). The concept of  curriculum assessment  has been defined broadly, among other suitable interpretations, as a “process of gathering and analyzing information from multiple sources in order to improve student learning in sustainable ways” ( Wolfe, Hill, & Evers, 2006 ). Curricular goals and the research devoted thereto vary widely from the appraisal of program-level learning outcomes, to success in post-graduate occupational placement (e.g.,  Chance & Peck, 2015; Maples, Greco, & Tanner, 2008 , respectively). A wealth of resources and methods for facilitating the evaluation of curricula are readily available, and are typically geared toward strategies for collecting and interpreting data (e.g., a complete curriculum assessment plan is accompanied by several surveys in the appendices of  Wolfe et al., 2006 ).

The evaluators of a curriculum are faced with the fundamental challenge of defining and measuring success. Usually, a collection of objectives that are deemed relevant to a given construct are introduced, and then assessment-metrics are utilized to gauge “success” by the extent to which these objectives are met. Revisions to the curriculum are then implemented with the purpose of improving levels of achievement toward selected goals.

Assessment-metrics provide a means by which statistical tools become available to evaluate a curriculum, and the exploration and analysis of these metrics is an active area of research (e.g.,  Ahmed & Bhatti, 2015; LaForge & Hodge, 2011; Mendez, Ochoa, Chiluiza, & de Wever, 2014 ). Unsurprisingly, statistical analyses make up the bulk of the quantitative literature on curriculum assessment. While these data-driven approaches expand on observations that reflect the nature of a curricular system, the present investigation is concerned with the general underlying structure that governs the outcomes of such analyses.

The contrast between data-driven approaches to curriculum assessment and the perspective taken in this study mimics that of the commonly accepted depictions of experimental physics, where the nature of a physical system is documented through empirical evidence, and theoretical physics, which seeks to explain the experimental findings through abstract mathematical models of the system. These mathematical models are often based on idealized prototypes of real-world objects. The physical existence of such a prototype need not be experimentally provable, but its theoretical existence is valued in accordance with its capacity to approximate that which is known to exist.

In this paper, the purely-mathematical notion of a “curriculum space” is developed. Concepts from graph theory and order theory are applied to illuminate the interplay between learners and the tasks that they are required to complete in a curriculum. The focus is placed on curricular properties rather than the characteristics of learners; in fact, learners are regarded as being boundlessly intelligent (although, not omniscient), which is conducive to isolating curricular effects on student-success. At least in part, the investigation is offered in response to an apparent demand for a precise language in which to communicate aspects of curricular structure that impact student-achievement. The manner in which prerequisite courses are structured within a curriculum is of particular interest.

The theory is developed from the ground up, beginning with primitive notions (including undefined terms and assumptions), and gradually moving toward rigorous definitions and theorems. Most of the main results are found in Sections  3 and 4 , with the majority belonging to the latter. From a purely order-theoretic perspective, most of Section  4  is independent of the investigations in Sections  2 and 3  (see the second paragraph of Section  4 ), and readers who are primarily interested in the structure of partially ordered sets are invited to forgo these two sections (although, see  Theorem 3.6 ).

The work in Sections  2 and 3  is driven by the idea that there are sometimes many different ways to perform a task, so that one’s ability to complete a given task may not be necessary for, but merely contributes toward the realization of one’s ability to complete a subsequent task (see the discussion at the beginning of Section  2.2 ). A mathematical portrayal of this notion is offered in Section  2  and, under the ensuing “contributing relation”, it is observed that arbitrary collections of tasks cannot, at the outset, be assumed to be partially ordered (cf.  Remark 2.4  and  Example 3.3 ). Section  2  supplies contextual understanding of the abstract mathematical model that is presented in Section  3 , and these sections serve to enable order theory for the applicability of Sections  4 and 5  (where tasks of a curriculum are always assumed to be partially ordered).

In Section  2 , the premises of the theory, and the mathematical objects that serve as prototypes of learners, curricula, courses, and the tasks that learners encounter in a curriculum are introduced. The concept of a task “contributing” toward a learner’s ability to complete another task is given formal meaning ( Definition 2.3 ). Also, circumstances that influence whether a learner is adequately supported by a curriculum are considered ( Condition 1 ).

In Section  3 , the concepts from Section  2  are translated into graph-theoretic terms. A “curriculum space” is defined to be a bipartite graph whose neighborhoods satisfy special conditions that reflect the premises introduced in Section  2  ( Definition 3.1 ). Curricula can then be regarded as particular collections of subsets from within a curriculum space, and it is shown that the tasks associated with “adequate curricula” (which, essentially, are self-contained curricula) are partially ordered according to a rule that respects the “contributing relation” ( Theorem 3.4 ). Moreover, the mathematical qualities of curriculum spaces are considered in their own right, and it is proved that every partially ordered set can be realized from the partial order induced by the tasks of an adequate curriculum ( Theorem 3.6 ).

Section  4  addresses the structure of prerequisite courses of a curriculum. A condition is presented for a prerequisite-structure to be “compatible” with the structure of the tasks covered by the curriculum ( Condition 2 ), and prerequisite-structures that satisfy this condition are completely characterized ( Theorems 4.7 and 4.8 ). Also, a greedy algorithm ( Algorithm 1 ) is given for categorizing courses according to “levels”, providing a rule for the construction of “curriculum maps” that respect compatible prerequisite-structures. The study concludes in Section  5 , where obstacles related to curriculum standards are considered, and are left open to further investigation.

References on graph theory and order theory can be found in  Bollobás (1998)  and  Davey and Priestley (2002) , respectively.

The theories in Sections  4 and 5  are given under the assumption that the “tasks” of a curriculum can be partially ordered in a relevant and meaningful way. In particular, it is expected that a rule has already been decided concerning what it means for the completion of a task to “contribute” toward a learner’s ability to complete another task. A mathematical description of one possible rule is offered in  Definition 2.3 . Subsequently, properties (premise ( 
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                      ) and  Condition 1 ) that tasks and curricula should obey are identified which, in Section  3 , are shown to be essential for the realization of partial orders that respect this rule. Ultimately, the intention is to establish arguments in support of implementing partially ordered sets as task-structures in the analyses presented in Sections  4 and 5 . The reader may wish to proceed to these sections, and refer to the following groundwork as needed.

To begin, define a  mental event  to be a unit consisting of all simultaneously occurring mental processes that make up a learner’s mental composition at a given moment, where “mental processes” are loosely taken to include thought, perception, decision, insight, etc. Also, a “task” can be regarded as an objective whose completion is enabled by the realization of a set of mental events. Throughout, the guiding sense of “achievement” will be marked by a learner’s aptitude for completing a task, but there will never be a need to recognize whether the learner has ever completed the task (this can be understood, perhaps, by considering a learner who is able to add any two integers between 1 and 1,000,000, but certainly has not added every possible pair of such integers).

By accepting broad and unspecific interpretations of “mental process” and “task”, it is hoped that the resulting theory will be general enough to sustain whatever definitions a cognitive scientist might put into effect. Also, notions of “intelligence” will not be considered, and there will be no difficulties in regarding a “learner” as a generic entity without limitations on its capacity for cognitive experience. Moreover, the following premises are accepted in this work.

Remark 2.1 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (1) 
                                     In deciding what mathematical objects should represent real-world learners, there would be some loss associated with settling for undefined elements without any imposition of further qualities. It is important to note that distinct elements of  
                                           μ 
                                         need not be thought of as mental representatives of distinct individuals. Instead, the learning progression of an individual should be thought to culminate in a subset of  
                                           μ 
                                         (in fact, a chain in  
                                           μ 
                                         under inclusion). Hence, aside from the primary effect of framing the elements  
                                           
                                              α 
                                              ∈ 
                                              μ 
                                           
                                         as emblems of real-world learners, the description “collections of mental events” is merely a mnemonic whose intuitive meaning serves to maintain the dynamic nature of an individual’s mental state. Specific mental events that constitute the elements of  
                                           μ 
                                        , however, will have no role in the development of the forthcoming mathematical arguments. 
                                  
                                  
                                     (2) 
                                     Although the relationship between  
                                           
                                              α 
                                              ∈ 
                                              μ 
                                           
                                         and  
                                           
                                              x 
                                              ∈ 
                                              τ 
                                           
                                         expressed in premise ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                              
                                                 2 
                                              
                                           
                                        ) by the phrase “ 
                                           α 
                                         is sufficient to complete  
                                           x 
                                        ” will not be rigorously defined, it should instinctively imply that a learner who realizes the set  
                                           α 
                                         of mental events has acquired the aptitude to complete the task  
                                           x 
                                        . When such a set has been realized by a learner, it will be said that the learner is  sufficient to complete  
                                        
                                           x 
                                        .

                                     The last assertion in premise (

                                        ) is given to avoid trivialities involving unachievable tasks. In particular, it may be regarded as a condition on the set  
                                           τ 
                                         rather than on the set  
                                           μ 
                                        .

Real-world learners are depicted by the members of  
                            μ

                            τ 
                          represent objectives that these learners may become capable of completing. Intuitively, a “curriculum” involves “courses” of tasks. Therefore, we adopt the following definition.

Definition 2.2

                                  τ 
                               , and any element of  
                                  C 
                                will be called a  course .

A program’s prerequisite-structure can be affected by a variety of factors. In managing the challenges associated with learning, a prerequisite course may be assigned to endow learners with familiarity of concepts that will be assumed in a subsequent course, or perhaps its purpose is just to promote a level of maturity to facilitate success in a subsequent course. Ultimately, if a course  
                            
                               
                                  c 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          is a prerequisite for a course  
                            
                               
                                  c 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         , then the completion of tasks in  
                            
                               
                                  c 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          should somehow contribute to a learner’s preparedness to successfully complete tasks in  
                            
                               
                                  c 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         .

How does one determine whether the completion of a task  
                            
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               τ 
                            
                          “contributes” to a learner’s preparedness to complete a task  
                            y 
                         ? In practice, it is sometimes a matter of common sense (e.g., multiplying integers should certainly contribute toward preparation for multiplying rational numbers), and simple metrics are often suitable to make judgments about the effectiveness of a contributing task (e.g., if data indicate that learners complete task  
                            y 
                          at a higher rate after completing the task  
                            x 
                         , then the administrators of a program may readily accept a proposal to incorporate  
                            x 
                          in a course that is prerequisite to a course containing  
                            y 
                         ). But what is the underlying structure of  
                            
                               ( 
                               μ 
                               , 
                               τ 
                               ) 
                            
                          that is reflected by such metrics?

If it is difficult or impossible to complete  
                            y 
                          prior to the completion of  
                            x 
                          then the rate of success in completing  
                            y 
                          among those who have not completed  
                            x 
                          should be near zero (e.g., one might expect this to be the case with the tasks of multiplying integers and multiplying rational numbers, as every integer is a rational number). More precisely, the inclusion  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                            
                          may be reflected by such a low rate of success, implying with distinct certitude that  
                            x 
                          contributes to preparedness (and is, in fact, necessary) for the completion of  
                            y 
                         . In less extreme scenarios, there may be alternative tasks that could contribute toward the completion of  
                            y 
                         , not all of which must be completed by a learner prior to the completion of  
                            y 
                          (e.g., in the previous illustration, replace “multiplying rational numbers” with, say, “counting entries in rectangular arrays of given dimensions”). In such cases, a fair amount of success in completing  
                            y 
                          in the absence of  
                            x 
                          could be recorded while data suggest, nevertheless, that the completion of  
                            x 
                          contributes toward preparedness for completing  
                            y 
                         . Given alternative means of preparation, the necessity of  
                            x 
                          exists, perhaps, only under restrictions against other tasks that contribute toward preparedness for completing  
                            y 
                         .

To establish a more inclusive definition of “contributing tasks”, we proceed under the rationale that the removal of “alternative tasks” should magnify the impact of the remaining tasks, and aid in the identification of tasks that contribute without being necessary. Thus, in asserting that a task  
                            
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               τ 
                            
                          contributes toward preparedness for completing a task  
                            
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               τ 
                            
                         , it will be implied that there exists a collection  
                            
                               T 
                               ⊆ 
                               τ 
                            
                          of tasks such that, if  
                            
                               α 
                               ∈ 
                               μ 
                            
                          is not sufficient to complete any of the tasks in  
                            T 
                         , then  
                            α 
                          cannot be sufficient to complete  
                            y 
                          without being sufficient to complete  
                            x 
                          (e.g., if “multiplying integers” contributes toward “counting entries in rectangular arrays of given dimensions”, then  
                            T 
                          might include the task of “constructing rectangular arrays”). More precisely, we have the following definition.

                            x 
                         , will prepare a learner for the completion of  
                            y 
                         .

Remark 2.4 
                            It is clear that  
                                  ∼ 
                                is reflexive (e.g., let  
                                  
                                     T 
                                     = 
                                     ∅ 
                                  
                                and apply premise ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               )), but we offer no suggestion that it is either antisymmetric or transitive (see  Example 3.3 ). On the other hand, note that  
                                  ∼ 
                                is more coarse than the  necessity partial order  on  
                                  τ 
                               ; that is, the partial order  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≲ 
                                     
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                  
                                on  
                                  τ 
                                defined by  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≲ 
                                        
                                        
                                           τ 
                                        
                                     
                                     y 
                                  
                                if and only if  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                     
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                (where antisymmetry follows by  Convention 1 ).

It is clear that  
                                  ∼ 
                                is reflexive (e.g., let  
                                  
                                     T 
                                     = 
                                     ∅ 
                                  
                                and apply premise ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               )), but we offer no suggestion that it is either antisymmetric or transitive (see  Example 3.3 ). On the other hand, note that  
                                  ∼ 
                                is more coarse than the  necessity partial order  on  
                                  τ 
                               ; that is, the partial order  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≲ 
                                     
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                  
                                on  
                                  τ 
                                defined by  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≲ 
                                        
                                        
                                           τ 
                                        
                                     
                                     y 
                                  
                                if and only if  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                     
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                (where antisymmetry follows by  Convention 1 ).

Further intuition is gained by considering the interpretation of  
                            x 
                          
                         not  contributing to  
                            y 
                         . By the following result,  
                            x 
                          does not contribute to  
                            y 
                          if and only if for every  
                            
                               α 
                               ∈ 
                               μ 
                            
                          that is sufficient to complete both  
                            x 
                          and  
                            y 
                          there exists  
                            
                               β 
                               ∈ 
                               μ 
                            
                          such that the collection of tasks that  
                            β 
                          is sufficient to complete is a subset of that which  
                            α 
                          is sufficient to complete, and yet  
                            β 
                          is sufficient to complete  
                            y 
                          without being sufficient to complete  
                            x 
                          (so that one may argue that success in becoming sufficient to complete  
                            y 
                          is never impacted by whether one is sufficient to complete  
                            x 
                         ).

                                     ∈ 
                                     τ

The final premise from which the abstract model of Section  3  will follow is provided below, and has an essential role in establishing partial orders on the tasks of curricula (in particular, it will arise in the verification of antisymmetry in the proof of  Theorem 3.4 ). Intuitively, it implies that if the ability to complete a task  
                            x 
                          facilitates one’s ability to complete another task  
                            y 
                         , then one may learn to complete  
                            x 
                          before learning to complete  
                            y 
                         .

( 
                                     
                                        
                                           P 
                                        
                                        
                                           3 
                                        
                                     
                                  ) 
                               If  
                                     
                                        x 
                                        ,

Learners who are adequately supported within a curriculum should be able to achieve sufficiency to complete tasks that are contained in the curriculum without being required to complete tasks that are missing from the curriculum (although, see  Remark 2.7 (2)). More formally, if  
                            
                               y

Remark 2.7 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (1) 
                                     By setting  
                                           
                                              T 
                                              = 
                                              τ 
                                              ∖ 
                                              ⋃ 
                                              C 
                                           
                                        , note that  Condition 1  is equivalent to the condition “ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    μ 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    y 
                                                 
                                              
                                              ∖ 
                                              ⋃ 
                                              
                                                 { 
                                              
                                              
                                                 
                                                    μ 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    z 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           | 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              z 
                                              ∈ 
                                              τ 
                                              ∖ 
                                              ⋃ 
                                              C 
                                              
                                                 } 
                                              
                                              ≠ 
                                              ∅ 
                                           
                                         for every  
                                           
                                              y 
                                              ∈ 
                                              ⋃ 
                                              C 
                                           
                                        ”. While it may seem absurd to require that a learner exists who is unable to complete tasks from outside of a curriculum, it is important to recall that the elements of  
                                           μ 
                                         are prototypes, and the condition reflects a theoretical scenario that is conducive to measuring a curriculum’s capacity to support learners that satisfy minimum conditions. 
                                  
                                  
                                     (2) 
                                     Strictly speaking, a curriculum might have to contain tasks appropriate to be completed in, say, kindergarten in order to satisfy the condition of being adequate. While there are no theoretical concerns with such a requirement, it may be favorable to put the condition into a context that is closer to what is typically permissible in real-world curricular development. Academic programs usually have some form of admissions criteria consisting of tasks contained in a subset  
                                           
                                              A 
                                              ⊆ 
                                              τ 
                                           
                                        , and a curriculum  
                                           C 
                                         is then developed so that  
                                           
                                              ⋃ 
                                              C 
                                              ⊆ 
                                              τ 
                                              ∖ 
                                              A 
                                           
                                        . Alternatively, a curriculum  
                                           C 
                                         may be regarded as having an “admissions course”  
                                           
                                              A 
                                              ∈ 
                                              C 
                                           
                                         (intuitively,  
                                           
                                              C 
                                              ∖ 
                                              
                                                 { 
                                                 A 
                                                 } 
                                              
                                           
                                         represents the collection of courses that would be offered by a program, while  
                                           A 
                                         is a collection of tasks that students have already achieved sufficiency to complete upon admittance into the program). The reader is free to adopt the latter perspective without forfeiting the integrity of the theory presented in this work.

Strictly speaking, a curriculum might have to contain tasks appropriate to be completed in, say, kindergarten in order to satisfy the condition of being adequate. While there are no theoretical concerns with such a requirement, it may be favorable to put the condition into a context that is closer to what is typically permissible in real-world curricular development. Academic programs usually have some form of admissions criteria consisting of tasks contained in a subset  
                                           
                                              A 
                                              ⊆ 
                                              τ 
                                           
                                        , and a curriculum  
                                           C 
                                         is then developed so that  
                                           
                                              ⋃ 
                                              C 
                                              ⊆ 
                                              τ 
                                              ∖ 
                                              A 
                                           
                                        . Alternatively, a curriculum  
                                           C 
                                         may be regarded as having an “admissions course”  
                                           
                                              A 
                                              ∈ 
                                              C 
                                           
                                         (intuitively,  
                                           
                                              C 
                                              ∖ 
                                              
                                                 { 
                                                 A 
                                                 } 
                                              
                                           
                                         represents the collection of courses that would be offered by a program, while  
                                           A 
                                         is a collection of tasks that students have already achieved sufficiency to complete upon admittance into the program). The reader is free to adopt the latter perspective without forfeiting the integrity of the theory presented in this work.

We close this section by presenting a modification of  
                            ∼ 
                          that will be useful in establishing partial orders on a curriculum  
                            C 
                         . Suppose that  
                            
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                            
                          such that  
                            x 
                          contributes to  
                            y 
                         . There exists  
                            
                               T 
                               ⊆ 
                               τ 
                            
                          such that  
                            
                               ∅ 
                               ≠ 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                               
                               ∖ 
                               ⋃ 
                               
                                  { 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     z 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         
                            | 
                         
                         
                            
                               z 
                               ∈ 
                               T 
                               
                                  } 
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . If it happens that  
                            
                               T 
                               ⊆ 
                               τ 
                               ∖ 
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                            
                         , then a learner who becomes sufficient to complete  
                            y 
                          without becoming sufficient to complete  
                            x 
                          must become sufficient to complete tasks outside of the curriculum (namely, tasks which can be found in  
                            T 
                         ). In this case, with respect to  
                            
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                            
                         , the task  
                            x 
                          may be regarded as “necessary” for  
                            y 
                         . Thus, we arrive at the following definition.

Definition 2.8 
                            If  
                                  C 
                                is a curriculum then let  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≲ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⋃ 
                                        C 
                                     
                                  
                                be the relation on  
                                  
                                     ⋃ 
                                     C 
                                  
                                defined by  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≲ 
                                        
                                        
                                           ⋃ 
                                           C 
                                        
                                     
                                     y 
                                  
                                if and only if  
                                  
                                     ∅ 
                                     ≠ 
                                     
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                     
                                     ∖ 
                                     ⋃ 
                                     
                                        {

                                  | 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     z 
                                     ∈ 
                                     T 
                                     
                                        }

                                           x 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                for some  
                                  
                                     T 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     τ 
                                     ∖ 
                                     ⋃ 
                                     C 
                                  
                               .

If  
                                  C 
                                is a curriculum then let  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≲ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⋃ 
                                        C 
                                     
                                  
                                be the relation on  
                                  
                                     ⋃ 
                                     C 
                                  
                                defined by  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≲ 
                                        
                                        
                                           ⋃ 
                                           C 
                                        
                                     
                                     y 
                                  
                                if and only if  
                                  
                                     ∅ 
                                     ≠

Note that  
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  C 
                               
                            
                          is a refinement of the relation  
                            ∼ 
                          (restricted to  
                            
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                            
                         ), while it is more coarse than the restriction of the “necessity” partial order  
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  τ 
                               
                            
                          defined in  Remark 2.4 . Of course, if  
                            
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                               = 
                               τ 
                            
                          then  
                            
                               T 
                               ⊆ 
                               τ 
                               ∖ 
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                            
                          if and only if  
                            
                               T 
                               = 
                               ∅ 
                            
                         , from which it follows that  
                            
                               “ 
                               
                                  
                                     ≲ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⋃ 
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                            
                         ” and “ 
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  τ 
                               
                            
                         ” are the same relation (so that the notation “ 
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  τ 
                               
                            
                         ” defined in  Remark 2.4  agrees with  Definition 2.8 ). For adequate  
                            C 
                         , it is straightforward to check that  
                            
                               x 
                               
                                  
                                     ≲ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⋃ 
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                               y 
                            
                          if and only if  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                               
                               ∖ 
                               ⋃ 
                               
                                  { 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     z 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         
                            | 
                         
                         
                            
                               z 
                               ∈ 
                               τ 
                               ∖ 
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                               
                                  } 
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . In the next section, it is proved (in greater generality) that if  
                            C 
                          is an adequate curriculum then  
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  C 
                               
                            
                          is a partial order on  
                            
                               ⋃ 
                               C 
                            
                         
 ( Theorem 3.4 ), but  
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  C 
                               
                            
                          need not be a partial order if  
                            C 
                          is not adequate ( Example 3.3 ).

Before considering the notion of a “prerequisite-structure” on a curriculum (which is the focus of Section  4 ), the concepts from Section  2  will be abstracted in order to obtain a general curriculum theory that recognizes tasks that contribute without being necessary. By establishing partial orders on tasks that respect the “contributing relation”, the results in this section show that the relevance of Sections  4 and 5  to such a curriculum theory can be mathematically supported. In fact, it is shown that partially ordered sets can be arbitrarily chosen as prototypes of task-structures of adequate curricula, so that the theory in the forthcoming sections can be carried out in the general setting of partially ordered sets without risk of considering structures that are not realizable.

To proceed, it will be important to recall some basic order and graph-theoretic concepts. Let  
                         
                            ( 
                            P 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       be a partially ordered set. If  
                         
                            p 
                            ∈ 
                            P 
                         
                       then let

                                           x 
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≲ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ⋃ 
                                                 C 
                                              
                                           
                                           y 
                                        
                                      if and only if  
                                        
                                           ∅ 
                                           ≠ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 N 
                                              
                                              
                                                 G 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              y 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ∖ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 N 
                                              
                                              
                                                 G 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              T 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ⊆ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 N 
                                              
                                              
                                                 G 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              x 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                        
                                      for some  
                                        
                                           T 
                                           ⊆ 
                                           τ 
                                           ∖ 
                                           ⋃ 
                                           C 
                                        
                                     .

The next example shows that the above definitions are not vacuous.

It is easy to check that if  
                         C 
                       is adequate and  
                         
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ∈ 
                            ⋃ 
                            C 
                         
                       then  
                         
                            x

                               y 
                               )

                               ∖

                               C 
                               )

                                  N 
                               
                               
                                  G 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       (cf. the last paragraph in Section  2 ). The following theorem shows that  
                         
                            ( 
                            C 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  C 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       is a partially ordered set when  
                         C 
                       is adequate. Moreover, for learners  
                         α 
                       who are not sufficient to complete any tasks from outside of  
                         
                            ⋃ 
                            C 
                         
                      , it shows that the collection of tasks that  
                         α 
                       is sufficient to complete forms a semi-ideal of  
                         
                            ( 
                            ⋃ 
                            C 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  C 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       (an observation that will be needed in Section  5 ).

Theorem 3.4 
                         
                            The following statements hold for every adequate curriculum  
                            
                               C 
                            
                            . 
                         
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (1) 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ≲ 
                                           
                                           
                                              ⋃ 
                                              C 
                                           
                                        
                                      
                                     is a partial order on  
                                     
                                        
                                           ⋃ 
                                           C 
                                        
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     (2) 
                                  
                                  
                                     If  
                                     
                                        
                                           α 
                                           ∈ 
                                           μ 
                                           ∖ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 N

Remark 3.5 
                         Suppose that

                                  C 
                                  = 
                                  τ

                                        τ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             for every

                                  ∈ 
                                  μ

                                  y

                                  τ

                                  ∈ 
                                  τ 
                               
                             such that  
                               
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≲ 
                                     
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               
                            .

Ordered structures associated with knowledge spaces have been studied in their own right, yielding interesting contributions to both pure and applied mathematics (e.g.,  Suck, 2003, 2004 ). The final result of this section verifies that every partially ordered set can be realized as  
                         
                            ( 
                            ⋃ 
                            C 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  ≲ 
                               
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  C 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       for some adequate curriculum  
                         C 
                      . Consequently, arbitrary partial orders can be employed to illustrate phenomena that arise in curriculum spaces (e.g., see  Examples 4.3 and 4.4 ).

                            If

                                  , 
                                  ≤

                            .
